nodes.label: brAIn project
nodes.description: Causal knowledge graph CLI backed by Kuzu (embedded graph DB). Stores the causal structure extracted from documents — not the text itself. Unit of storage is an atomic assertion (node + typed relation + evidence), not a paragraph. Claude does the semantic extraction; brain.py does validation, deduplication, and persistence. Global CLI accessible from any directory via the 'brain' wrapper. UI: React frontend (port 5173) + FastAPI backend (port 8000) for visual graph exploration.

nodes.label: Causal structure storage
nodes.description: The founding design choice: store the causal structure a document describes, not the document text itself. Once extracted, the source document is disposable — all useful information lives in nodes (comprehensive descriptions) and typed relations (evidence explaining why the relationship holds). Contrast with RAG systems that store text chunks and re-read them at query time, paying token cost and losing structural reasoning.

nodes.label: Agentic tool-use extraction
nodes.description: Extraction mode where Claude calls the query_graph tool on demand before creating each node, checking whether an equivalent node already exists in the graph. If found, reuses its exact ID; if not, creates a new node. This prevents node fragmentation without loading the full graph into context. The graph is long-term memory; the context window is working memory — only retrieve what you need, when you need it. Empirically: 32% node reuse rate on a 2-doc test (neural_network + backpropagation).

nodes.label: query_graph tool
nodes.description: Anthropic tool-use function exposed to Claude during agentic extraction. Input: {topic: string}. Calls context_for_topic(conn, topic, limit=10, neighbors=5) — full-text search on label/description + 1-hop neighborhood. Returns match_count, matched nodes with outgoing/incoming edges. Claude uses this to decide: reuse existing ID, or mint new node. Implemented in lib/query.py:context_for_topic.

nodes.label: Kuzu graph DB
nodes.description: Embedded graph database (single process, no server) with strict Cypher schema. Stores graph at graph/kuzu_db/. Exclusive write lock — only one writer at a time; read_only=True for concurrent reads (FastAPI backend). Schema: one node table Node with id (PK), label, type, description, importance, sources[], created_at, updated_at; one rel table Rel with type, confidence, evidence, sources[], evidences[], created_at, updated_at.

nodes.label: Open node types
nodes.description: Node types are a reference vocabulary (concept, entity, mechanism, algorithm, artifact, property, event, claim, process, person) but any non-empty string is accepted at ingest time. Unknown types are logged to extension_requests.jsonl for human review but the node is created. Rationale: coherence comes from the lookup-before-create protocol (query_graph), not type enforcement. Contrast with strict relation types.

nodes.label: Strict relation types
nodes.description: Relation types are a hard whitelist: causes, prevents, requires, enables, precedes, contradicts (causal core) + is_a, part_of, instance_of, similar_to, property_of (structural) + related_to (fallback, max 5% of edges). Any type outside this list is rejected and logged. Rationale: the causal vocabulary must not drift — it is what makes the graph traversable and meaningful. Without it, the graph degrades into a flat tag cloud.

nodes.label: doc_id idempotency
nodes.description: Re-ingesting a payload with an existing doc_id is safe and idempotent. The ingester first purges all contributions of that doc_id (removes from Rel.sources/evidences, deletes edge if sources becomes empty; nodes are kept), then inserts the new payload. Enables the auto-update pattern: when a project file changes, re-ingest its doc_id and the graph reflects the new state cleanly without manual cleanup.

nodes.label: Project context pattern
nodes.description: Convention for using brAIn as permanent context for software projects. doc_id = 'project_<name>_<aspect>', sources include 'project:<name>'. At session start: brain find <project> to load context. While working: when Claude learns something structurally important (architectural decisions, causal relationships, design tradeoffs), it extracts to graph. Query at any time: brain query MATCH (n:Node) WHERE any(s IN n.sources WHERE s CONTAINS '<name>') RETURN n.label, n.type. Global brain command accessible from any directory.

nodes.label: ID slugification
nodes.description: The ingest CLI rewrites node IDs to match slugify(label): lowercase, non-alphanumeric replaced by underscore, leading/trailing underscores stripped. If the declared ID differs from slugify(label), the ID is rewritten and logged. Implication: node labels in JSON payloads must avoid special characters (parentheses, slashes, dots) or relations referencing those nodes will silently skip. Confirmed pitfall: 'Reliability sigmoid G(f,d)' → id 'reliability_sigmoid_g_f_d'; 'H/C ratio v0.5' → 'h_c_ratio_v0_5'. Rule documented in SKILL.md Step 2: use plain ASCII labels, run 'brain find <label>' after ingest to confirm actual id before writing rels.

nodes.label: brain.py CLI
nodes.description: Main entry point at /home/marius/Documents/brAIn/brain.py. Commands: init (create Kuzu schema), ingest <path> (validate + persist JSON payload), find <query> (full-text search), show <id> (node detail + relations), causes <id> (upstream chain), effects <id> (downstream chain), paths <a> <b> (all paths between two nodes), query <cypher> (raw Cypher), stats (counts by type), audit (quality metrics: orphans, related_to ratio, low-confidence edges), merge <a> <b> (merge two nodes). Global wrapper at ~/.local/bin/brain.

rels.evidence: Storing causal structure instead of text is the founding design thesis of brAIn — without it, the project is just another RAG system.
Agentic tool use is the mechanism that makes the graph grow coherently without fragmentation — Claude queries before creating, so nodes converge rather than multiply.
The query_graph tool is what Claude calls to check for existing nodes during extraction — it is the implementation of the agentic lookup-before-create protocol.
Kuzu is the storage engine of brAIn — all nodes, relations, and metadata are persisted in graph/kuzu_db/.
A fixed causal vocabulary (causes, prevents, requires, enables...) is what makes the stored structure traversable and meaningful — free-form relation types would make the graph unnavigable.
Open node types allow brAIn to work across any domain without requiring pre-definition — the vocabulary grows naturally as new domains are ingested.

Idempotent re-ingestion by doc_id is what makes the auto-update pattern possible: when a project file changes, re-ingest its doc_id and the graph cleanly reflects the new state without manual diff or cleanup.
The project context pattern is the primary use case for brAIn beyond document ingestion — making project knowledge permanently queryable across sessions.
ID slugification is enforced by the ingest CLI on every ingestion — it rewrites IDs that don't match slugify(label) and logs the rewrite.
Agentic extraction must use simple ASCII labels to avoid ID rewrites that would cause relations to reference wrong IDs and be silently skipped — a known pitfall when writing JSON payloads manually.
brain.py is the main CLI entrypoint — all user interactions with the graph go through it.
